headline: Gig Workers Act 872: SOCSO Coverage Extended, EPF Retirement Still Missing
context: Malaysia's Gig Workers Bill 2025 (Act 872), passed by the Dewan Rakyat in August and Dewan Negara on 9 September 2025, creates the first dedicated legal framework for gig workers. SOCSO self-employment protection is extended; EPF retirement contributions are not included.

cards.big: Act 872 creates Malaysia's first dedicated gig-worker legal framework, extending SOCSO self-employment coverage to platform workers.
cards.sub: Passed by Dewan Rakyat August 2025, Dewan Negara 9 September 2025. A 1.25% per-transaction deduction from gig-worker income funds the SOCSO contribution.

cards.big: Mandatory SOCSO accident and disability protection: platforms must register workers and remit contributions on their behalf.
cards.sub: Categories include p-hailing, e-hailing, freelancers, online services and other platform-mediated services. Platforms must inform workers of agreed terms.

cards.big: EPF retirement contributions are NOT included in this first phase. The long-tail income gap remains unaddressed.
cards.sub: Voluntary i-Saraan covered ~10.7% of self-employed workers by 2022. The new mandatory scheme does not extend to retirement.

cards.big: DOSM Q1 2025: 17.23 million labour force, 16.70 million employed, 3.45 million informal. Gig workers fall inside the informal universe.
cards.sub: MOHR cited 'over 1.2 million' during Bill passage; later MOHR FAQ cites MDEC 2024 data of ~1.64 million on digital platforms. Methodologies differ.

cards.big: The 'no social-security protection' framing is now narrower. The remaining gap is retirement adequacy, which platforms still do not fund.
Like adding seatbelts but skipping the crumple zone. The cheapest accident protection is in place; long-term retirement protection is not.
cards.sub: Accident insurance is the easiest gig-economy reform. Retirement adequacy is the hardest, and the new framework does not touch it.

cards.big: Phase 1 establishes mandatory platform-collected SOCSO deductions. The open Phase 2 question is whether retirement-savings coverage follows, and on what timeline.
